actinic_keratosis: Distinguished by rough, scaly plaques from chronic sun exposure, unlike the nodular or pigmented lesions of other classes.
Actinic keratosis is a precancerous lesion with superficial keratinocyte atypia, setting it apart from benign and malignant tumors.
This class shows irregular borders and sandpaper-like texture, unlike the smooth appearance of nevi or vascular lesions.
Unlike basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis does not form pearly nodules or ulcerate.
Actinic keratosis is easily separated from melanoma by its superficial, non-invasive nature.
basal_cell_carcinoma: Characterized by pearly or translucent nodules with telangiectasia, unlike the flat or scaly lesions of other classes.
Basal cell carcinoma grows slowly and rarely metastasizes, distinguishing it from aggressive melanoma.
This class often shows rolled borders and arborizing vessels, not seen in benign keratosis or nevi.
Unlike actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma is invasive and can cause local tissue destruction.
Basal cell carcinoma is set apart from vascular lesions by its malignant epithelial origin.
benign_keratosis: Distinguished by a warty or waxy appearance with a stuck-on look, unlike the nodular or pigmented lesions of other classes.
Benign keratosis is non-cancerous and shows uniform color and surface texture, unlike melanoma or basal cell carcinoma.
This class is well-demarcated and raised, setting it apart from flat or infiltrative lesions.
Unlike actinic keratosis, benign keratosis is not precancerous and lacks keratinocyte atypia.
Benign keratosis is easily separated from vascular lesions by the absence of blood vessel proliferation.
dermatofibroma: Characterized by a firm, hyperpigmented papule with a central dimple, unlike the nodular or scaly lesions of other classes.
Dermatofibroma is benign and does not transform into cancer, distinguishing it from actinic keratosis and melanoma.
This class shows a peripheral pigment network and central white scar, not seen in nevi or keratosis.
Unlike vascular lesions, dermatofibroma does not blanch with pressure or show a spongy texture.
Dermatofibroma is set apart from melanoma by its stable, non-aggressive behavior.
melanocytic_nevus: Distinguished by well-circumscribed, symmetric lesions with uniform color, unlike the asymmetry of melanoma.
Melanocytic nevi are benign and stable, setting them apart from malignant melanoma and basal cell carcinoma.
This class is defined by benign proliferation of melanocytes, not seen in keratosis or vascular lesions.
Unlike actinic keratosis, nevi do not show scaly or rough surfaces.
Melanocytic nevi are easily separated from vascular lesions by the absence of blood vessel proliferation.
melanoma: Characterized by asymmetry, irregular borders, and multiple colors, melanoma is distinct from all benign classes.
Melanoma is aggressive and shows atypical pigment network, unlike the uniform appearance of nevi or keratosis.
This class may show regression, ulceration, or variegated pattern, not seen in basal cell carcinoma or dermatofibroma.
Unlike actinic keratosis, melanoma is deeply invasive and has high metastatic potential.
Melanoma is set apart from vascular lesions by its malignant melanocyte origin and pigmentation.
vascular_lesion: Distinguished by red, purple, or blue papules or plaques with a spongy or compressible texture, unlike all other classes.
Vascular lesions blanch with pressure and show homogeneous or lacunar patterns, not seen in keratosis or nevi.
This class is defined by proliferation of blood vessels, unlike the epithelial or melanocytic origin of other lesions.
Unlike melanoma or basal cell carcinoma, vascular lesions are usually benign and non-pigmented.
Vascular lesions are easily separated from dermatofibroma by their color and compressibility.
